title: Chess Tournament

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: A single-elimination knockout tournament has 2^n players, with skills ranked 1 > 2 > ... > 2^n (the higher-ranked player ALWAYS wins). The bracket is a uniformly random pairing.
rules: Single elimination: lose once and you're out.
There are n rounds; the final is round n with two players.
setup.question: What is the probability that players 1 and 2 meet in the FINAL?

learningObjectives: Recognize that player 1 always wins, so always reaches the final.
Reduce the question to: 'is player 2 in the OTHER half of the bracket from player 1?'
Count slots in player 1's half vs the other half.

stages.label: Player 1 always reaches the final
stages.prompt: Why is player 1 guaranteed to reach the final?

options.text: Player 1 has the highest skill, so wins every match. They survive all n-1 prior rounds and reach round n (the final).
Random chance gives them a 50% shot.
They might lose in early rounds.

stages.explanation: Better-skilled always wins and player 1 has the highest skill. So player 1 wins every match and reaches the final automatically.

stages.label: Reduce to bracket placement
stages.prompt: For player 2 to meet player 1 in the final, where must player 2 be?

options.text: In the OPPOSITE half of the bracket from player 1 (so they only meet in the final).
Must be in player 1's same half.

stages.explanation: If player 2 is in the same half as player 1, they meet in some earlier round (and player 2 loses, never reaching the final). If they're in OPPOSITE halves, player 2 wins their half (skill #2 beats skills 3..2^n) and meets player 1 in the final.

stages.label: Count the slots
stages.prompt: There are 2^n total slots. Player 1 occupies one slot. The OTHER 2^n - 1 slots are split equally between player 1's half (2^(n-1) - 1 slots) and the OTHER half (2^(n-1) slots). What's P(player 2 in the other half)?
stages.explanation: Player 2 lands in one of the 2^n - 1 remaining slots uniformly. The 'other half' contains 2^(n-1) of those slots. So P = 2^(n-1) / (2^n - 1).

stages.label: Concrete check at n = 3
stages.prompt: For 8 players (n = 3), compute P(1 and 2 meet in the final).

stages.label: Commit and observe limit
stages.prompt: What does P approach as n -> infinity (very large tournaments)?
stages.explanation: For large n, 2^(n-1) / (2^n - 1) ≈ 2^(n-1) / 2^n = 1/2. Makes intuitive sense: the chance player 2 lands in the 'other half' approaches 50% as bracket sizes grow.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What's the probability that players 1 and 3 meet in the final? (Hint: player 3 must beat player 2 too -- impossible.)
What's the probability that players 1 and 2 meet in round k (for k < n)?
Verify by direct conditional probability: at each round k = 1, ..., n-1, P(don't meet | hadn't met) = 2*(2^(n-k) - 1) / (2^(n-k+1) - 1). Show the product gives 2^(n-1) / (2^n - 1).
